## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Economy of London](/wiki/Economy_of_London "Economy of London")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Banco_de_Inglaterra%2C_Londres%2C_Inglaterra%2C_2014-08-11%2C_DD_141.JPG/250px-Banco_de_Inglaterra%2C_Londres%2C_Inglaterra%2C_2014-08-11%2C_DD_141.JPG)](/wiki/File:Banco_de_Inglaterra,_Londres,_Inglaterra,_2014-08-11,_DD_141.JPG)

The [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), on [Threadneedle Street](/wiki/Threadneedle_Street "Threadneedle Street"), is the central bank of the United Kingdom.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Paternoster_Square.jpg/250px-Paternoster_Square.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paternoster_Square.jpg)

[Paternoster Square](/wiki/Paternoster_Square "Paternoster Square"), since 2004 the home of the [London Stock Exchange](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange")

The City of London vies with New York City's [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") for the distinction of the world's pre-eminent financial centre. The [London Stock Exchange](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange") ([shares](/wiki/Share_capital "Share capital") and [bonds](/wiki/Bond_(finance) "Bond (finance)")), [Lloyd's of London](/wiki/Lloyd%27s_of_London "Lloyd's of London") (insurance) and the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") are all based in the city.[[73]](#cite_note-NG-73) Over 500 banks have offices in the city. The [Alternative Investment Market](/wiki/Alternative_Investment_Market "Alternative Investment Market"), a market for trades in [equities](/wiki/Capital_stock "Capital stock") of smaller firms, is a recent development. In 2009, the City of London accounted for 2.4% of UK GDP.[[74]](#cite_note-citylondon-74)

London's foreign exchange market has been described by Reuters as 'the crown jewel of London's financial sector'.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Of the $3.98 trillion daily global turnover, as measured in 2009, trading in London accounted for around $1.85 trillion, or 46.7% of the total.[[74]](#cite_note-citylondon-74) The [pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling"), the currency of the United Kingdom, is globally the fourth-most traded currency[[76]](#cite_note-76) and the fourth most held [reserve currency](/wiki/Reserve_currency "Reserve currency").[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Canary Wharf, a few miles east of the City in Tower Hamlets, which houses many banks and other institutions formerly located in the Square Mile, has since 1991 become another centre for London's financial services industry. Although growth has continued in both locations,[*[ambiguous](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and there have been relocations in both directions, the Corporation has come to realise that its planning policies may have been causing financial firms to choose Canary Wharf as a location.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2022, 12.3% of City of London residents had been granted non-domicile status in order to avoid their paying tax in the UK.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

### Headquarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Headquarters")]

Many major global companies have their headquarters in the city, including [Aviva](/wiki/Aviva "Aviva"),[[79]](#cite_note-79) [BT Group](/wiki/BT_Group "BT Group"),[[80]](#cite_note-80) [Lloyds Banking Group](/wiki/Lloyds_Banking_Group "Lloyds Banking Group"),[[81]](#cite_note-81) [Quilter](/wiki/Quilter_plc "Quilter plc"), [Prudential](/wiki/Prudential_plc "Prudential plc"),[[82]](#cite_note-82) [Schroders](/wiki/Schroders_plc "Schroders plc"),[[83]](#cite_note-83) [Standard Chartered](/wiki/Standard_Chartered "Standard Chartered"),[[84]](#cite_note-84) and [Unilever](/wiki/Unilever_PLC "Unilever PLC").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

A number of the world's largest law firms are headquartered in the city, including four of the [Magic Circle](/wiki/Magic_Circle_(law_firms) "Magic Circle (law firms)") law firms ([Allen & Overy](/wiki/Allen_%26_Overy "Allen & Overy"), [Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer](/wiki/Freshfields_Bruckhaus_Deringer "Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer"), [Linklaters](/wiki/Linklaters "Linklaters") and [Slaughter & May](/wiki/Slaughter_%26_May "Slaughter & May")), as well as other firms such as [Ashurst](/wiki/Ashurst_LLP "Ashurst LLP"), [DLA Piper](/wiki/DLA_Piper "DLA Piper"), [Eversheds Sutherland](/wiki/Eversheds_Sutherland "Eversheds Sutherland"), [Herbert Smith Freehills](/wiki/Herbert_Smith_Freehills "Herbert Smith Freehills") and [Hogan Lovells](/wiki/Hogan_Lovells "Hogan Lovells").

### Other sectors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Other sectors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Barbican_Centre_City_of_London.jpg/250px-Barbican_Centre_City_of_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barbican_Centre_City_of_London.jpg)

Barbican Centre

Whilst the financial sector, and related businesses and institutions, continue to dominate, the economy is not limited to that sector. The legal profession has a strong presence, especially in the west and north (i.e., towards the [Inns of Court](/wiki/Inns_of_Court "Inns of Court")). Retail businesses were once important, but have gradually moved to the [West End of London](/wiki/West_End_of_London "West End of London"), though it is now Corporation policy to encourage retailing in some locations, for example at [Cheapside](/wiki/Cheapside "Cheapside") near St Paul's. The city has a number of visitor attractions, mainly based on its historic heritage as well as the [Barbican Centre](/wiki/Barbican_Centre "Barbican Centre") and adjacent [Museum of London](/wiki/Museum_of_London "Museum of London"), though tourism is not at present a major contributor to the city's economy or character. The city has many pubs, bars and restaurants, and the "night-time" economy does feature in the [Bishopsgate](/wiki/Bishopsgate "Bishopsgate") area, towards [Shoreditch](/wiki/Shoreditch "Shoreditch"). The meat market [at Smithfield](/wiki/Smithfield_Market "Smithfield Market"), wholly within the city, continues to be one of London's main markets (the only one remaining in central London) and the country's largest [meat market](/wiki/Butcher "Butcher"). In the east is [Leadenhall Market](/wiki/Leadenhall_Market "Leadenhall Market"), a fresh food market that is also a visitor attraction.

### Retail and residential

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Retail and residential")]

The trend for purely office development is beginning to reverse as the Corporation encourages residential use, albeit with development occurring when it arises on windfall sites. The city has a target of 90 additional dwellings per year.[[86]](#cite_note-86) Some of the extra accommodation is in small pre-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"), which are not suitable for occupation by the large companies which now provide much of the city's employment. Recent residential developments include "the Heron", a high-rise residential building on the Milton Court site adjacent to the Barbican, and the [Heron Plaza](/wiki/Heron_Tower#Heron_Plaza "Heron Tower") development on Bishopsgate is also expected to include residential parts.

Since the 1990s, the City has diversified away from near exclusive office use in other ways. For example, several hotels and the first [department store](/wiki/Department_store "Department store") opened in the 2000s. A shopping centre was more recently opened at [One New Change](/wiki/One_New_Change "One New Change"), [Cheapside](/wiki/Cheapside "Cheapside") (near St Paul's Cathedral) in October 2010, which is open seven days a week. However, large sections remain quiet at weekends, especially in the eastern section, and it is quite common to find shops, [pubs](/wiki/Public_house "Public house") and cafes closed on these days.

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

### Historic buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Historic buildings")]

Fire, bombing and post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") redevelopment have meant that the city, despite its history, has fewer intact historic structures than one might expect. Nonetheless, there remain many dozens of (mostly Victorian and Edwardian) fine buildings, typically in historicist and [neoclassical style](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture"). They include the [Monument to the Great Fire of London](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Great_Fire_of_London "Monument to the Great Fire of London") ("the Monument"), [St Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Cathedral "St Paul's Cathedral"), [the Guildhall](/wiki/Guildhall,_London "Guildhall, London"), the [Royal Exchange](/wiki/Royal_Exchange,_London "Royal Exchange, London"), [Dr. Johnson's House](/wiki/Dr._Johnson%27s_House "Dr. Johnson's House"), [Mansion House](/wiki/Mansion_House,_London "Mansion House, London") and a [great many churches](/wiki/Category:Churches_in_the_City_of_London "Category:Churches in the City of London"), many designed by [Sir Christopher Wren](/wiki/Sir_Christopher_Wren "Sir Christopher Wren"), who also designed St Paul's.

[Prince Henry's Room](/wiki/Prince_Henry%27s_Room "Prince Henry's Room") and [2 King's Bench Walk](/wiki/2_King%27s_Bench_Walk "2 King's Bench Walk") are notable historic survivors of heavy bombing of [the Temple](/wiki/Temple,_London "Temple, London") area, which has largely been rebuilt to its historic form. Another example of a bomb-damaged place having been restored is [Staple Inn](/wiki/Staple_Inn "Staple Inn") on Holborn. A few small sections of the Roman [London Wall](/wiki/London_Wall "London Wall") exist, for example near the Tower of London and in the Barbican area. Among the twentieth-century [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_buildings "Listed buildings") are [Bracken House](/wiki/Bracken_House,_London "Bracken House, London"), the first post World War II buildings in the country to be given statutory protection, and the whole of the [Barbican](/wiki/Barbican_Estate "Barbican Estate") and [Golden Lane Estate](/wiki/Golden_Lane_Estate "Golden Lane Estate").

The [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London") is not in the city, but is a notable visitor attraction which brings tourists to the southeast of the city. Other landmark buildings with historical significance include the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), the [Old Bailey](/wiki/Old_Bailey "Old Bailey"), the [Custom House](/wiki/Custom_House,_City_of_London "Custom House, City of London"), [Smithfield Market](/wiki/Smithfield,_London "Smithfield, London"), [Leadenhall Market](/wiki/Leadenhall_Market "Leadenhall Market") and [St Bartholomew's Hospital](/wiki/St_Bartholomew%27s_Hospital "St Bartholomew's Hospital"). Noteworthy contemporary buildings include a number of modern high-rise buildings (see section below) as well as the [Lloyd's building](/wiki/Lloyd%27s_building "Lloyd's building").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Royal_Exchange_Panorama.jpg/960px-Royal_Exchange_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Exchange_Panorama.jpg)

The Bank of England (left) and the Royal Exchange (centre) are two of the many significant buildings in the City of London.

A growing number of tall buildings and skyscrapers are principally used by the financial sector. Almost all are situated in the eastern side around [Bishopsgate](/wiki/Bishopsgate "Bishopsgate"), [Leadenhall Street](/wiki/Leadenhall_Street "Leadenhall Street") and [Fenchurch Street](/wiki/Fenchurch_Street "Fenchurch Street"), in the financial core of the city. In the north there is a smaller cluster comprising the [Barbican Estate](/wiki/Barbican_Estate "Barbican Estate")'s three tall residential towers and the commercial [CityPoint](/wiki/CityPoint "CityPoint") tower. In 2007, the 100 m (328 ft) tall [Drapers' Gardens](/wiki/Drapers%27_Gardens "Drapers' Gardens") building was demolished and replaced by a shorter tower.

[DLR](/wiki/Docklands_Light_Railway "Docklands Light Railway") trains link the City directly to Canary Wharf.

The [Docklands Light Railway](/wiki/Docklands_Light_Railway "Docklands Light Railway") (DLR [![Docklands Light Railway](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/DLR_roundel_%28no_text%29.svg/20px-DLR_roundel_%28no_text%29.svg.png)](/wiki/Docklands_Light_Railway "Docklands Light Railway")) has two termini in the city: Bank and [Tower Gateway](/wiki/Tower_Gateway_DLR_station "Tower Gateway DLR station"). The DLR links the City directly to the [East End](/wiki/East_End_of_London "East End of London"). Destinations include Canary Wharf and [London City Airport](/wiki/London_City_Airport "London City Airport").[[88]](#cite_note-:0-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)

The [Elizabeth line](/wiki/Elizabeth_line "Elizabeth line") (constructed by the [Crossrail](/wiki/Crossrail "Crossrail") project) runs east–west underneath the City of London. The line serves two stations in or very near the City – [Farringdon](/wiki/Farringdon_station "Farringdon station") and Liverpool Street – which additionally serves the Barbican and Moorgate areas. Elizabeth line services link the City directly to destinations such as Canary Wharf, [Heathrow Airport](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport "Heathrow Airport"), and the [M4 Corridor](/wiki/M4_corridor "M4 corridor") high-technology hub (serving [Slough](/wiki/Slough "Slough") and [Reading](/wiki/Reading,_Berkshire "Reading, Berkshire")).[[90]](#cite_note-91)

The city is served by a frequent [Thameslink](/wiki/Thameslink "Thameslink") rail service which runs north–south through London. Thameslink services call at Farringdon, [City Thameslink](/wiki/City_Thameslink_railway_station "City Thameslink railway station"), and London Blackfriars. This provides the city with a direct link to key destinations across London, including [Elephant & Castle](/wiki/Elephant_%26_Castle_railway_station "Elephant & Castle railway station"), [London Bridge](/wiki/London_Bridge_station "London Bridge station"), and [St Pancras International](/wiki/St_Pancras_railway_station "St Pancras railway station") (for the [Eurostar](/wiki/Eurostar "Eurostar") to [mainland Europe](/wiki/Mainland_Europe "Mainland Europe")). There are also regular, direct trains from these stations to major destinations across [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") and [the South East](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England"), including [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford "Bedford"), [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton"), [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), [Gatwick Airport](/wiki/Gatwick_Airport "Gatwick Airport"), [Luton Airport](/wiki/Luton_Airport "Luton Airport"), and [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough "Peterborough").[[91]](#cite_note-:1-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Greater_Anglia_745010_Colchester.jpg/250px-Greater_Anglia_745010_Colchester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greater_Anglia_745010_Colchester.jpg)

The [Stansted Express](/wiki/Stansted_Express "Stansted Express") departs from [Liverpool Street Station](/wiki/Liverpool_Street_station "Liverpool Street station") in the city and runs directly to [Stansted Airport](/wiki/London_Stansted_Airport "London Stansted Airport") in Essex.

There are several "[London Terminals](/wiki/London_Terminals "London Terminals")"[[91]](#cite_note-:1-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) in the city:

* [London Blackfriars](/wiki/Blackfriars_station "Blackfriars station") – Thameslink services and some [Southeastern](/wiki/Southeastern_(train_operating_company) "Southeastern (train operating company)") services to South East London and [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent").
* [London Cannon Street](/wiki/Cannon_Street_station "Cannon Street station") – Southeastern services to South East London and Kent.
* [London Fenchurch Street](/wiki/Fenchurch_Street_railway_station "Fenchurch Street railway station") – [c2c](/wiki/C2c "C2c") services along the [Thames Estuary](/wiki/Thames_Estuary "Thames Estuary") towards East London, south [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), and [Southend](/wiki/Southend-on-Sea "Southend-on-Sea").
* [London Liverpool Street](/wiki/Liverpool_Street_station "Liverpool Street station") – [Greater Anglia](/wiki/Greater_Anglia_(train_operating_company) "Greater Anglia (train operating company)") and some c2c services towards destinations in East London and East Anglia, including [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford,_London "Stratford, London"), Cambridge, [Chelmsford](/wiki/Chelmsford "Chelmsford"), [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich "Ipswich"), [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), Southend, and [Southend Airport](/wiki/London_Southend_Airport "London Southend Airport"). [Stansted Express](/wiki/Stansted_Express "Stansted Express") to [Stansted Airport](/wiki/London_Stansted_Airport "London Stansted Airport"). [London Overground](/wiki/London_Overground "London Overground") ([![Lea Valley lines](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Overground_roundel_%28no_text%29.svg/20px-Overground_roundel_%28no_text%29.svg.png)](/wiki/Lea_Valley_lines "Lea Valley lines"))[[93]](#cite_note-94) to destinations in north and east London including [Hackney Downs](/wiki/Hackney_Downs_railway_station "Hackney Downs railway station"), [Seven Sisters](/wiki/Seven_Sisters_station "Seven Sisters station"), [Walthamstow](/wiki/Walthamstow_Central_station "Walthamstow Central station"), [Chingford](/wiki/Chingford "Chingford"), [Enfield](/wiki/Enfield_Town "Enfield Town"), and [Cheshunt](/wiki/Cheshunt_railway_station "Cheshunt railway station").
* [Moorgate](/wiki/Moorgate_station "Moorgate station") – [Great Northern](/wiki/Thameslink,_Southern_and_Great_Northern_franchise "Thameslink, Southern and Great Northern franchise") towards [Finsbury Park](/wiki/Finsbury_Park_station "Finsbury Park station"), Enfield, and other destinations in North London and [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), including [Hertford](/wiki/Hertford "Hertford") and [Welwyn Garden City](/wiki/Welwyn_Garden_City "Welwyn Garden City").

All stations in the city are in [London fare zone 1](/wiki/London_fare_zone_1 "London fare zone 1").[[88]](#cite_note-:0-89)

Space taken vs numbers in City of London (transport)[[94]](#cite_note-95)

The national [A1](/wiki/A1_road_(Great_Britain) "A1 road (Great Britain)"), [A10](/wiki/A10_road_(England) "A10 road (England)") [A3](/wiki/A3_road "A3 road"), [A4](/wiki/A4_road_(England) "A4 road (England)"), and [A40 road](/wiki/A40_road "A40 road") routes begin in the city. The city is in the [London congestion charge](/wiki/London_congestion_charge "London congestion charge") zone, with the small exception on the eastern boundary of the sections of the A1210/A1211 that are part of the [Inner Ring Road](/wiki/Inner_Ring_Road,_London "Inner Ring Road, London"). The following bridges, listed west to east (downstream), cross the River Thames: [Blackfriars Bridge](/wiki/Blackfriars_Bridge "Blackfriars Bridge"), [Blackfriars Railway Bridge](/wiki/Blackfriars_Railway_Bridge "Blackfriars Railway Bridge"), [Millennium Bridge](/wiki/Millennium_Bridge_(London) "Millennium Bridge (London)") (footbridge), [Southwark Bridge](/wiki/Southwark_Bridge "Southwark Bridge"), [Cannon Street Railway Bridge](/wiki/Cannon_Street_Railway_Bridge "Cannon Street Railway Bridge") and London Bridge; [Tower Bridge](/wiki/Tower_Bridge "Tower Bridge") is not in the city. The city, like most of central London, is well [served by buses](/wiki/Buses_in_London "Buses in London"), including night buses. Two bus stations are in the city, at [Aldgate](/wiki/Aldgate_bus_station "Aldgate bus station") on the eastern boundary with Tower Hamlets, and at [Liverpool Street](/wiki/Liverpool_Street_bus_station "Liverpool Street bus station") by the railway station.
However although the London Road Traffic Act 1924 removed from existing local authorities the powers to prevent the development of road passengers transport services within the London Metropolitan Area, the City of London retained most such powers. As a consequence, neither Trolleybus nor Green Line Coach services were permitted to enter the City to pick up or set down passengers. Hence the building of Aldgate (Minories) Trolleybus and Coach station as well as the complex terminal arrangements at Parliament Hill Fields. This restriction was removed by the Transport Act 1985.

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Cycling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Cycle_superhighway_6_at_Blackfriars.jpg/250px-Cycle_superhighway_6_at_Blackfriars.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cycle_superhighway_6_at_Blackfriars.jpg)

[Cycleway 6](/wiki/List_of_cycle_routes_in_London "List of cycle routes in London") runs between [Elephant & Castle](/wiki/Elephant_and_Castle "Elephant and Castle") and [Kentish Town](/wiki/Kentish_Town "Kentish Town"), passing through the City of London between Blackfriars and Farringdon.

[Cycling infrastructure](/wiki/Cycling_infrastructure "Cycling infrastructure") in the city is maintained by the City of London Corporation and [Transport for London](/wiki/Transport_for_London "Transport for London") (TfL).[[95]](#cite_note-:2-96)

* [Cycle Superhighway 1](/wiki/List_of_cycle_routes_in_London "List of cycle routes in London") runs from [Tottenham](/wiki/Tottenham "Tottenham") to the city. It is a signposted cycle route, passing through [Stoke Newington](/wiki/Stoke_Newington "Stoke Newington") and [Hackney](/wiki/Hackney,_London "Hackney, London") before entering the City south of Old Street.
* [Cycle Superhighway 2](/wiki/List_of_cycle_routes_in_London "List of cycle routes in London") runs from [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford,_London "Stratford, London") to the city, via [Bow](/wiki/Bow,_London "Bow, London"), [Mile End](/wiki/Mile_End "Mile End"), and [Whitechapel](/wiki/Whitechapel "Whitechapel"). The route enters the city near Aldgate. The route runs primarily on segregated [cycle track](/wiki/Cycle_track "Cycle track").
* [Cycleway 3](/wiki/Cycle_Superhighway_3 "Cycle Superhighway 3") is an east–west [bike freeway](/wiki/Bike_freeway "Bike freeway") through the city. The route runs along the southern rim of the city, following the route of the Thames. Eastbound, Cycleway 3 provides cyclists with a direct, signposted cycle link to [Shadwell](/wiki/Shadwell "Shadwell"), [Poplar](/wiki/Poplar,_London "Poplar, London") and Canary Wharf, and [Barking](/wiki/Barking,_London "Barking, London"). The route runs Westbound on traffic-free track to [Lancaster Gate](/wiki/Lancaster_Gate "Lancaster Gate") via [Parliament Square](/wiki/Parliament_Square "Parliament Square"), [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"), and [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London").
* [Cycleway 6](/wiki/List_of_cycle_routes_in_London "List of cycle routes in London") runs north–south through the city on traffic-free cycle track. The track passes [Farringdon Station](/wiki/Farringdon_station "Farringdon station"), the [Holborn Viaduct](/wiki/Holborn_Viaduct "Holborn Viaduct"), [Ludgate Circus](/wiki/Ludgate_Circus "Ludgate Circus"), [Blackfriars station](/wiki/Blackfriars_station "Blackfriars station"), and [Blackfriars Bridge](/wiki/Blackfriars_Bridge "Blackfriars Bridge"). Northbound, the route passes through [Clerkenwell](/wiki/Clerkenwell "Clerkenwell"), [Bloomsbury](/wiki/Bloomsbury "Bloomsbury"), [King's Cross](/wiki/Kings_Cross,_London "Kings Cross, London"), and [Kentish Town](/wiki/Kentish_Town "Kentish Town"). The route southbound carries cyclists to [Elephant and Castle](/wiki/Elephant_and_Castle "Elephant and Castle").
* [Cycle Superhighway 7](/wiki/List_of_cycle_routes_in_London "List of cycle routes in London") begins in the City at an interchange with Cycleway 3. It leaves the City over [Southwark Bridge](/wiki/Southwark_Bridge "Southwark Bridge") and provides cyclists with an unbroken, signposted route to [Colliers Wood](/wiki/Colliers_Wood "Colliers Wood") via Elephant and Castle, [Clapham](/wiki/Clapham "Clapham"), and [Tooting](/wiki/Tooting "Tooting"), amongst other destinations.
* [Quietway 11](/wiki/List_of_cycle_routes_in_London "List of cycle routes in London") is a northbound continuation of Cycleway 7. It is a signposted cycle route which runs from Southwark Bridge to [Hoxton](/wiki/Hoxton "Hoxton"), via the Barbican and Moorgate.

The [Sandander Cycles](/wiki/Santander_Cycles "Santander Cycles") and Beryl bike sharing systems operate in the City of London.[[95]](#cite_note-:2-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97)

### River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: River")]

One [London River Services](/wiki/London_River_Services "London River Services") pier is on the Thames in the city, [Blackfriars Millennium Pier](/wiki/Blackfriars_Millennium_Pier "Blackfriars Millennium Pier"), though the [Tower Millennium Pier](/wiki/Tower_Millennium_Pier "Tower Millennium Pier") lies adjacent to the boundary near the Tower of London. One of the [Port of London](/wiki/Port_of_London "Port of London")'s 25 [safeguarded wharves](/wiki/Safeguarded_wharf "Safeguarded wharf"), [Walbrook Wharf](/wiki/Walbrook_Wharf "Walbrook Wharf"), is adjacent to Cannon Street station, and is used by the corporation to transfer waste via the river. Swan Lane Pier, just upstream of London Bridge, is proposed to be replaced and upgraded for regular passenger services, planned to take place in 2012–2015. Before then, Tower Pier is to be extended.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

There is a public riverside walk along the river bank, part of the [Thames Path](/wiki/Thames_Path "Thames Path"), which opened in stages – the route within the city was completed by the opening of a stretch at [Queenhithe](/wiki/Queenhithe "Queenhithe") in 2023.[[98]](#cite_note-99) The walk along Walbrook Wharf is closed to pedestrians when waste is being transferred onto barges.

### Travel to work (by residents)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Travel to work (by residents)")]

According to a survey conducted in March 2011, the methods by which employed residents 16–74 get to work varied widely: 48.4% go on foot; 19.5% via light rail, (i.e. [the Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground"), [DLR](/wiki/Docklands_Light_Railway "Docklands Light Railway"), etc.); 9.2% work mainly from home; 5.8% take the train; 5.6% travel by bus, minibus, or coach; and 5.3% go by bicycle; with just 3.4% commuting by car or van, as driver or passenger.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Sir_John_Cass%27s_Foundation_Primary_School_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Sir_John_Cass%27s_Foundation_Primary_School_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_John_Cass%27s_Foundation_Primary_School_(cropped).jpg)

[The Aldgate School](/wiki/The_Aldgate_School "The Aldgate School") (using its former name)

The city is home to a number of higher education institutions including: the [Guildhall School of Music and Drama](/wiki/Guildhall_School_of_Music_and_Drama "Guildhall School of Music and Drama"), the [Cass Business School](/wiki/Cass_Business_School "Cass Business School"), [The London Institute of Banking & Finance](/wiki/The_London_Institute_of_Banking_%26_Finance "The London Institute of Banking & Finance") and parts of three of the [universities in London](/wiki/Universities_in_London "Universities in London"): the [Maughan Library](/wiki/Maughan_Library "Maughan Library") of [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London") on [Chancery Lane](/wiki/Chancery_Lane "Chancery Lane"), the business school of [London Metropolitan University](/wiki/London_Metropolitan_University "London Metropolitan University"), and a campus of the [University of Chicago Booth School of Business](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Booth_School_of_Business "University of Chicago Booth School of Business"). [The College of Law](/wiki/University_of_Law "University of Law") has its London campus in [Moorgate](/wiki/Moorgate "Moorgate"). Part of [Barts and The London School of Medicine and Dentistry](/wiki/Barts_and_The_London_School_of_Medicine_and_Dentistry "Barts and The London School of Medicine and Dentistry") is on the Barts hospital site at West Smithfield.

The city has only one directly maintained primary school, [The Aldgate School](/wiki/The_Aldgate_School "The Aldgate School") (formerly Sir John Cass's Foundation Primary School) at [Aldgate](/wiki/Aldgate "Aldgate")[[100]](#cite_note-101) (ages 4 to 11). It is a Voluntary-Aided (VA) [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") school, maintained by the Education Service of the City of London.

City residents send their children to schools in neighbouring [Local Education Authorities](/wiki/Local_Education_Authority "Local Education Authority"), such as [Islington](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Islington "London Borough of Islington"), [Tower Hamlets](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Tower_Hamlets "London Borough of Tower Hamlets"), [Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster") and [Southwark](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Southwark "London Borough of Southwark").

The City controls three independent schools, [City of London School](/wiki/City_of_London_School "City of London School") (a boys' school) and [City of London School for Girls](/wiki/City_of_London_School_for_Girls "City of London School for Girls") in the city, and the [City of London Freemen's School](/wiki/City_of_London_Freemen%27s_School "City of London Freemen's School") (co-educational day and boarding) in [Ashtead](/wiki/Ashtead "Ashtead"), Surrey. The City of London School for Girls and City of London Freemen's School have their own preparatory departments for entrance at age seven. It is the principal sponsor of The [City Academy, Hackney](/wiki/City_Academy,_Hackney "City Academy, Hackney"), [City of London Academy Islington](/wiki/City_of_London_Academy_Islington "City of London Academy Islington"), and [City of London Academy, Southwark](/wiki/City_of_London_Academy,_Southwark "City of London Academy, Southwark").[[101]](#cite_note-102)

### Public libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Public libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/KCLMaughanLibrary2.jpg/250px-KCLMaughanLibrary2.jpg)](/wiki/File:KCLMaughanLibrary2.jpg)

The [Maughan Library](/wiki/Maughan_Library "Maughan Library"), [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London"), located on [Chancery Lane](/wiki/Chancery_Lane "Chancery Lane")

Libraries operated by the Corporation include three lending libraries; Barbican Library, Shoe Lane Library and Artizan Street Library and Community Centre. Membership is open to all – with one official proof of address required to join.

Guildhall Library, and City Business Library are also public reference libraries, specialising in the history of London and business reference resources.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

## Money laundering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_London&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Money laundering")]

The City of London's role in illicit financial activity such as [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering") has earned the financial hub sobriquets such as 'The Laundromat' and 'Londongrad'.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

In May 2024, the UK's then deputy foreign secretary, [Andrew Mitchell](/wiki/Andrew_Mitchell "Andrew Mitchell"), said that 40% of the dirty money in the world goes through London and crown dependencies.[[104]](#cite_note-105)
